entry.title: ChannelNewsAsia: 28 May, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Tricking: Putting the 'art' in martial arts | Interactive
  • UN rights envoy defends controversial China visit
  • Putin says he's willing to discuss resuming Ukrainian grain shipments
  • Second seed Medvedev trounces Kecmanovic in straight sets for fourth round spot
  • Perez pips Leclerc in final Monaco practice
  • McLaren say Norris will be fine for Monaco race day
  • French Open day seven
  • Sinner punishes wasteful McDonald to book fourth round spot
  • US doctors reconsider Pfizer's Paxlovid for lower-risk COVID patients
  • Swiatek runs into trouble but reaches French Open last 16
  • Western Utd dethrone Melbourne City to take Australian title
  • PREVIEW-Egypt’s Al Ahly seek to cement African club dominance
  • Cash-strapped Sri Lanka gets Russian oil to ease shortages
  • Road Trip On Keikyu Line - Part 1
  • Climate change effect on Peruvian glaciers debated in German court
  • Disease experts call on WHO, governments for more action on monkeypox
  • Russia shows off Zircon hypersonic cruise missile in test-launch at sea
  • Ukraine receives Harpoon missiles and howitzers, says defence minister
  • Indonesia rescuers search for 26 after boat capsizes
  • Victoria's Secret pays US$8.3 million settlement to sacked Thai workers
  • Beijing to relax COVID-19 curbs in some areas
  • Shanghai edges towards COVID-19 reopening as Beijing plans to ease curbs
  • Samoa signs China agreement amid South Pacific push
  • UN Security Council members fail to agree on Myanmar text
  • Japanese Red Army founder Shigenobu freed from prison
  • North Korea says new fever cases under 100,000 as COVID-19 fight heats up

  • US issues sanctions targeting North Korean weapons of mass destruction programme
  • Amazon investor proposal to review plastic use narrowly fails to clear
  • Home and away: What’s fuelling the rise of Singaporeans investing in property abroad?
  • Commentary: The death of globalisation has been greatly exaggerated
  • Recession fears? Some businesses in Singapore bracing for troubled times ahead
  • Transport Canada probing cause of Tesla fire in Vancouver
  • Binance registers with Italy's regulator amid plans to expand in Europe
  • Exclusive-HSBC clients query bank on climate, one to review engagement – sources
  • Not a surprise if Singapore detects monkeypox case in coming weeks: Ong Ye Kung
  • National silat coach Romadhon, 33, dies following car accident in Bali
  • 20 people evacuated after chlorine gas detected at Gambas Crescent
  • Workplace deaths tally for 2022 rises to 24 after man falls 9.5m through skylight
  • Commentary: Why cheating and verbal abuse incidents drive some to call for culprits to be fired
  • Period poverty exists in Singapore – so I helped start a non-profit that gives out free pads to women
  • Hanami Prawn Cracker and Chris' Homestyle Spring Onion Dip recalled over undeclared allergens
  • Chelsea sale to Boehly consortium to be completed on Monday
  • No conflict over' Uncle Toni' as Auger-Aliassime faces Nadal
  • Chelsea reach final agreement for sale of club to Boehly-led consortium
  • Hwang to miss two June friendlies due to military training
  • Revenge fuels Rajasthan, Gujarat look to cap dream IPL season
  • Duplantis takes pole vault gold at Prefontaine Classic, Mahuchikh wins high jump
  • Force down Canes to stay alive, Blues edge Waratahs
  • Liverpool to play for Ukraine people in Champions League final, says Klopp
  • Liverpool and Real Madrid ready for Champions League final rematch
  • Dalian Pro earn CSL reprieve following Chongqing withdrawal
  • Reports: Lakers decide on Darvin Ham as next head coach
  • Iran police tear-gas protesters after building collapse: Reports
  • Ukraine says 'everything' being done to defend Donbas from Russian onslaught
  • Turkey shows off drones at Azerbaijan air show
  • Texas school shooting: 11-year-old survivor smeared blood on herself to hide from gunman
  • French officials to use orcas sounds to guide lost killer whale back to sea
  • Trump urges end to gun-free school zones, easier confinement of 'deranged' people
  • WHO says 650 probable cases of acute child hepatitis reported so far
  • Mexican judge suspends bullfights in world's largest ring
CNA: Breaking News, Singapore News, World and Asia

entry.title: Washington Post: 28 May, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Russia-Ukraine war live updates: Russia claims capture of key transport hub, as Zelensky remains defiant
  • Founder of Japanese Red Army militant group leaves jail after 20 years
  • Putin’s war grinds on, with dissent from Russian hawks and peace-seekers
  • An ‘unprecedented’ effort to document war crimes in Ukraine. But what chance of justice?
  • Facing a power crisis and searing heat, India falls back on coal
  • U.S. is preparing to send long-range rocket systems to Ukraine
  • Israel faces test of anti-terror tactics with planned flag march
  • Russia takes territory in eastern Ukraine; U.S. to send long-range rocket systems
  • Canadian Supreme Court rules all killers must have chance at parole
  • Russia has incited genocide in Ukraine, independent experts conclude
  • Former Louvre president charged in art trafficking case
  • Pope, Anglican, Scottish church leaders to pray in Africa
  • Angelo Sodano, once-powerful Vatican prelate, dies at 94
  • Former Albanian President Bujar Nishani dies at 55
  • Tropical storm forms off Mexico's Pacific coast
  • Iran disperses crowd angry over building collapse killing 29
  • Show's over for famed cabaret show at France's Lido
  • UN experts: Libya's security threatened by foreign fighters
  • Shanghai lockdown: Residents demand release, and some get it
  • Fleeing the Russians: Evacuations are slow, arduous, fraught
  • 15 dead, 3 missing after torrential rains in southern China
  • Tiny school police force in Uvalde took charge, then failed to go in
  • Experts cast doubt on high-tech efforts to stop school shooters
  • Singer Don McLean leads wave of dropouts from NRA convention after Texas shooting
  • Post Politics Now: Speaking to midshipmen, Biden says Putin’s brutality toward Ukraine rallied Europe in opposition
  • Epic goods-buying spree wanes, as consumers ramp up services spending
  • Inside the race to master supersonic air travel
  • Before massacre, Uvalde gunman frequently threatened teen girls online
  • Putin’s Unconditional Surrender Should Not Be the Goal
  • How the Market Is Severely Punishing Growth Traps
  • Venice Has a 400-Year-Old Covid Monetary Lesson
  • Understanding IPEF and How It Counters China’s Clout
  • Police mistakes in Uvalde should not take focus off the real issue: guns
  • Let’s focus more on why people kill than what they use to do it
  • The Post endorses Katie O’Malley in Maryland’s attorney general Democratic primary
  • In occupied Kherson, Russia is imposing a grim, hopeless reality
  • Ask Eugene Robinson about the latest news around the U.S. and world
  • Chat with Perry Bacon Jr. about politics and beyond
  • Chat with Jennifer Rubin about her columns, politics, policy and more
  • Send a letter to the editor
  • Contact the reader representative
  • Submit an op-ed
Breaking news and analysis on politics, business, world national news, entertainment more. In-depth DC, Virginia, Maryland news coverage including traffic, weather, crime, education, restaurant reviews and more.

entry.title: Guardian: 28 May, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • French Open day seven: Tsitsipas in action, Swiatek and Medvedev through – live!
  • Leinster v La Rochelle: Champions Cup final – live!
  • Russia-Ukraine war: Russia claims capture of strategic town as fighting rages in east; Johnson and Zelenskiy hold talks – live
  • Champions League: Liverpool fans cross Channel on speedboat after flight cancelled
  • On my radar: Yuval Noah Harari’s cultural highlights
  • Revealed: Russia-linked superyachts ‘going dark’ to avoid tracking systems
  • Between Two Worlds review – Emmanuel Carrère’s jagged, furious tale of low-paid work
  • Tory discontent with Boris Johnson spreads as MPs fear losing seats
  • Revealed: Afghan journalists facing death threats and beatings, despite UK pledge to save them
  • ‘Our friends didn’t die in vain’: Sudan’s activists aim to topple military regime

  • Huma Abedin: ‘Hillary Clinton doesn’t give up. Her positivity is contagious’
  • Harry Styles: One Night Only review – the model of a modern pop star
  • One to watch: Rachel Chinouriri
  • The love song of TS Eliot: intense letters reveal the passion behind the pen
  • Roman Abramovich bids his farewell as Chelsea takeover nears confirmation
  • Elon Musk welcomes global recession: ‘it’s been raining money on fools for too long’ | Andrew Lawrence
  • When I found out about the brain tumour that would kill me, I faced my worst fears – but I chose to take it on
  • First case of monkeypox confirmed in Ireland
  • Victoria’s Secret pays sacked Thai workers $8.3m in ‘wage theft’ settlement
  • Toyah Willcox and Robert Fripp look back: ‘He came from the 60s, having multiple girlfriends at once’
  • Pavement return: ‘There are few artists not named Jagger or McCartney who have no financial worries’
  • Cannes 2022 week two roundup: a bloated Elvis and a brush with greatness
  • British MP’s son saves fellow volunteer while under fire in Ukraine
  • 26 people missing after ferry sinks in Indonesia
  • I never liked jigsaws – so what am I doing representing the US at the ‘puzzle Olympics’?
  • Ed Sheeran review – sentimental, standardised but potent stadium pop
  • Ivan Perisic accepts offer to join Spurs when Inter deal ends next month
  • Has Colombian left’s time come at last as ex-rebel closes in on presidency?
  • Will this fruit-picking robot transform agriculture?
  • Rewilding, or just a greenwashed land grab? It all depends on who benefits | Eleanor Salter
  • ‘Reflections make me think of mystery’: Almudena López Calafate’s best phone picture
  • School shooting strengthens case for guns, Donald Trump tells NRA
  • Australian man killed in Ukraine while providing humanitarian aid
  • Yotam Ottolenghi’s recipes for the perfect picnic
  • ‘I woke up and he was on top of me’: six women on being abused by fashion agent Jean-Luc Brunel
  • Books by women that every man should read: chosen by Ian McEwan, Salman Rushdie, Richard Curtis and more
  • Samoa signs China bilateral agreement during Pacific push by Beijing
  • Beekeepers and communists: how environmentalists started a global conversation
  • Liverpool chase defining high in blue-chip final against Real Madrid
  • IPL and Jos Buttler show ECB how to make cricket work as entertainment | Barney Ronay
  • Ambivalence about the Queen seems modern – but it’s actually a Victorian feeling | Ian Jack
  • ‘Outside space? That’s the beach’: restoring a 300-year-old fishing cottage
  • Pro-choice forces are working to keep abortion legal in Michigan with a ballot initiative
  • Queen’s platinum jubilee to be marked by 16,000 street parties across England
  • ‘High drama, with the lowest stakes’ – what really happened at the Wagatha Christie trial
  • Eat plants, try pilates and stay positive: how to keep your body younger than your years
  • ‘Empress of terror’: Japanese Red Army founder released from prison
  • That other American tradition: after a mass shooting, the media comes to town
  • Blind date: ‘I was so engrossed, I leaned over the candle and my sleeve caught on fire’
  • Kremlin mulls Nuremberg-style trials based on second world war tribunals
  • Jimmy Butler’s heroics spur Heat to victory and Game 7 decider against Celtics
  • Monkeypox outbreak could be just ‘the peak of the iceberg’, WHO warns
  • Ailing orca stuck in France’s River Seine to be lured to sea using drone with loudspeakers
  • Canada investigates after Tesla catches fire, forcing driver to ‘smash the window’
  • Pellet gun recovered after Toronto police shoot dead man ‘carrying rifle’
  • Trent Alexander-Arnold: ‘I always feel I can see things others can’t see’
  • French prosecutor says antisemitism suspected in murder of elderly man
  • America, how long will you sacrifice your children on the altar of gun worship? | Jonathan Freedland
  • Russia’s ‘cauldron’ tactic may be tipping Donbas battle in its favour
Latest international news, sport and comment from the Guardian

entry.title: South China Morning Post: 28 May, 2022
entry.summary: South China Morning Post: 28 May, 2022 published at 2022-05-28 14:00PM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Hong Kong’s next leader John Lee heads to Beijing to hand in list of proposed governing team, accept appointment letter
  • Hong Kong police probe suspected drowning after woman’s body found floating in Upper Shing Mun Reservoir
  • Off-duty Hong Kong police officer arrested for drink-driving after Tesla rams bakery, allegedly hits pedestrian
  • Hong Kong dog shelter struggling to provide as number of animals doubles while volunteers move abroad amid pandemic
  • Hong Kong uncovers Covid cluster in high-end Central nightclub as officials consider making arrivals take extra coronavirus test
  • New Hong Kong police anti-riot ‘Saber-toothed Tigers’ ready ahead of city’s 25th handover anniversary
  • National security law: Hong Kong must uphold mainland China’s socialist system, stamp out delusions of change from ‘street politics’, state official warns
  • Coronavirus: why Hong Kong remains Asia’s foremost arts hub despite tough Covid-19 curbs
  • As tourism reopens from Asia to Europe, here are the dos and don’ts of post-pandemic travel
  • How to repair Hong Kong’s battered reputation: first, restore confidence among the ‘honest brokers’
  • Number of Hong Kong pupils at UK private schools with parents also living in country triples following launch of BN(O) scheme
  • Centralcon’s Fo Tan housing project The Arles concludes with less than 2 per cent sold in latest offering
  • Hong Kong’s food trucks get new lease of life after timely deal with city’s arts hub, but vendors hope for project extension
  • Can government revamp bring Hong Kong closer to achieving its climate goals?
  • British MPs call for audit of property and assets held by Hong Kong and mainland Chinese officials
  • In the DRC, a Chinese-Australian battle for control of a massive untapped lithium lode
  • Coronavirus in China: Covid tests for the dead spark online mockery in Shenzhen
  • Chinese foreign ministry veteran tipped to take up top propaganda job
  • China-West rivalry hits the Pacific as Wang Yi comes calling with security deals
  • ‘Surprise, surprise’: Chinese man hides wedding ring inside future wife’s Kindle device for 7 years until their big day
  • China needs to ‘work with the rest of the world’ to stop cybercrime, trafficking
  • Daily cases drop to new low in Shanghai as city government vows to boost economy while maintaining zero Covid goal
  • China has discreetly taken 10 per cent of the world’s market for hydrogen fuel-cell vehicles. Here’s a look at the company leading the charge
  • Tencent restructures its news team as online censorship and competition with short video outfits such as Douyin grows in China
  • China pushes for smarter armed forces with education shake-up on path to modern military
  • US extends tariff exemption on imported Chinese surgical masks for 6 more months
  • China food security: Premier Li Keqiang says summer harvest ‘must go on’, despite virus controls
  • Germany denies China investment guarantees over human rights in Xinjiang
  • What’s the future of Web3? Meta and Animoca Brands face off over metaverse visions at Hong Kong webinar
  • Vanuatu declares climate emergency – ‘we are in danger now’ – PM says
  • Indonesia ferry sinks – at least 26 people still missing
  • Coronavirus: N Korea reports 88,500 more with Covid symptoms – disbelief over tiny death rate
  • Victoria’s Secret agrees to finance US$8.3 million settlement for laid-off Thai garment workers
  • China and Russia block UN Security Council statement on Myanmar crisis
  • Muck not yuck: Fly larvae could be solution to the Philippines’ landfill, food waste woes
  • Samoa signs bilateral agreement with China amid Beijing’s Pacific push
  • Cambodia’s reopened Siem Reap sets its sights on a ‘quality’ tourist resurgence
  • Keep the South China Sea free, Joe Biden tells US Navy graduates
  • ‘Hunger catastrophe looming’, Zelensky warns Jakarta town hall meeting, with Ukraine’s grain cut off from the world
  • Tourism in Asia is bouncing back, but can the rebound survive a global recession?
  • US hits North Korea with fresh sanctions over missile development
  • Malaysia’s Mahathir Mohamad says new US-led trade group IPEF intended to ‘isolate China’
  • Huddle around the TV to save power, Japanese families are told by government
  • Coronavirus: US patient group calls for recurring Covid-19 boosters to cut through vaccine apathy – take-up not enough to curb pandemic
  • As US reassures Gulf allies, China reaches out to Middle East amid rising oil prices
  • From Don McLean to Lee Greenwood, country music stars distance themselves from NRA after Texas massacre
  • Silvio Berlusconi’s bad break-up with Vladimir Putin throws light on Italy-Russia ties
  • Spanish LGBT groups wary of monkeypox stigma ahead of Europe’s largest pride parade
  • Texas massacre: Protesters rally outside NRA convention as Trump calls for teachers to be armed
  • London’s new Elizabeth Line is open – and it’s being run by Hong Kong’s MTR
  • Ukraine troops may retreat from eastern region as Russia advances
  • Texas school massacre: children phoned to beg for help as police waited in corridor
  • Can we avoid a global recession? That largely depends on the US
  • Nearly 200 cases of monkeypox in more than 20 countries, WHO says
  • Hong Kong homebuyers adopt wait-and-see approach amid rising mortgage rates and higher unemployment
  • Mainland and Hong Kong ETFs will join stock connect in move seen as ‘gift’ for 25th anniversary of handover
  • Cryptocurrency giant FTX ready with billions of dollars for acquisitions
  • Bitcoin breaks from stocks and keeps on falling as cryptocurrencies slide
  • How cutting-edge ‘robotic’ prosthetics developed for amputees could transform the able-bodied into augmented cyborgs
  • Sports Illustrated Swimsuit’s first Asian plus-size cover model Yumi Nu didn’t always love her size or her race
  • Hong Kong handover memories relived in 25th anniversary photo exhibition, from PLA troops entering the city to emotions and celebrations
  • No 7 on World’s Best Bar list, No 1 on Asia’s Best Bar list – Coa in Hong Kong: co-founder Jay Khan on his journey to opening the Mexican-inspired cocktail joint
  • ‘It is like a vitamin’: wave of mindfulness meditation reaches Chinese shores as working adults grapple with stressful lives and Covid-19
  • For Hong Kong craft brewery Young Master, canned highballs are just the beginning – the cocktail possibilities are endless, says founder
  • From a vertical gondola ride in Singapore to Bangkok’s downtown forest park, seven new Southeast Asian tourist attractions
  • NMIXX, rookie K-pop girl group, talk about single O.O, KCON in Chicago – their first overseas concert – and their edgy style
  • Photos of China’s vast Inner Mongolia region, and how global capitalism has made inroads, captured by native who finally woke up to his homeland’s beauty
  • Jung Woo-sung set for TV melodrama Say You Love Me, Ha Ji-won joins Kang Ha-neul, Youn Yuh-jung on Curtain Call – K-drama casting latest
  • How Hong Kong’s underground art scene broke overground: Banksy lit the fuse on a street art boom – now local talents are worth 10 times as much
  • Melania Trump’s favourite car collection: from the Mercedes-Benz SLR McLaren – a US$600,000 gift from Donald – and ‘The Beast’ presidential limousine Cadillac, to an Orange County Choppers bike
  • What is Scott Disick doing now – and how does he feel about Kourtney Kardashian’s marriage? While she married Travis Barker in Italy, he flirted with 23-year-old model Holly Scarfone on Instagram
  • Who’s the richest Stranger Things cast member? Net worths, ranked – Millie Bobby Brown, Finn Wolfhard and Noah Schnapp earn millions as child stars, but how does Winona Ryder’s iconic career compare?
SCMP.com, Hong Kong's premier online English daily provides exclusive up-to-date news, audio video news, podcasts, RSS Feeds, Blogs, breaking news, top stories, award winning news and analysis on Hong Kong and China.

entry.title: Asahi Shimbun: 28 May, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Coast Guard obtains warrant to seize sunken pleasure boat
  • More questions over ministry responses to statistics scandal
  • Former head of Japanese Red Army now free and apologetic

  • VOX POPULI: Another school shooting in U.S. When will this madness end?
The Asahi Shimbun is widely regarded for its journalism as the most respected daily newspaper in Japan. The English version offers selected articles from the vernacular Asahi Shimbun, as well as extensive coverage of cool Japan,focusing on manga, travel and other timely news.

Wired is a full-color monthly American magazine, published in both print and online editions, that reports on how emerging technologies affect culture, the economy and politics. Monthly edition, best run at the start of every month.

entry.content:
Articles in this issue:

  • The overlooked variable in animal studies: why diet makes a difference
  • Timbre in the timber: how I date ancient violins
  • Maternal enzyme reprograms paternal DNA for healthy offspring
  • Sinking diatoms trap silicon in deep seawater of acidified oceans
  • Breakthrough in teleportation furthers quantum network development
  • From the archive: Jamaican coral reefs, and indispensable photography
  • Volatile neurons unite to stabilize visual experience
  • Metabolic diversity drives cancer cell invasion
  • Evolution of Earth’s tectonic carbon conveyor belt
  • Defining the risk of SARS-CoV-2 variants on immune protection
  • In situ recording of Mars soundscape
  • Free-electron lasing with compact beam-driven plasma wakefield accelerator
  • Qubit teleportation between non-neighbouring nodes in a quantum network
  • Realizing repeated quantum error correction in a distance-three surface code
  • Demonstration of fault-tolerant universal quantum gate operations
  • Cilia metasurfaces for electronically programmable microfluidic manipulation
  • Cobalt-electrocatalytic HAT for functionalization of unsaturated C–C bonds
  • Enhanced silica export in a future ocean triggers global diatom decline
  • Hmx gene conservation identifies the origin of vertebrate cranial ganglia
  • Mosquito brains encode unique features of human odour to drive host seeking
  • Emergent reliability in sensory cortical coding and inter-area communication
  • Transcriptomic mapping uncovers Purkinje neuron plasticity driving learning
  • Extricating human tumour immune alterations from tissue inflammation
  • Intracellular lipid surveillance by small G protein geranylgeranylation
  • Intermittent PI3Kδ inhibition sustains anti-tumour immunity and curbs irAEs
  • PHGDH heterogeneity potentiates cancer cell dissemination and metastasis
  • Transcriptional coupling of distant regulatory genes in living embryos
  • Maternal inheritance of glucose intolerance via oocyte TET3 insufficiency
  • Molecular basis for the initiation of DNA primer synthesis
  • Author Correction: Fluctuating interaction network and time-varying stability of a natural fish community
  • Author Correction: The loss of ions from Venus through the plasma wake
  • Publisher Correction: Reproducible brain-wide association studies require thousands of individuals
Nature is a weekly international multidisciplinary scientific journal publishing peer-reviewed research in all fields of science and technology on the basis of its originality, importance, interdisciplinary interest, timeliness, accessibility, elegance and surprising conclusions. Nauture also provides rapid, authoritative, insightful and arresting news and interpretation of topical and coming trends affecting science, scientists and the wider public.

entry.title: Scientific American: June 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • U.S. Kids Are Falling behind Global Competition, but Brain Science Shows How to Catch Up
  • How the Brain 'Constructs' the Outside World
  • How Mammals Conquered the World after the Asteroid Apocalypse
  • Mysterious Fast Radio Bursts Are Finally Coming into Focus
  • Tuberculosis Is the Oldest Pandemic, and Poverty Makes It Continue

  • The Urgent Need to Improve Health Equity
  • Mental Health Care Should Be Available for All, Not a Luxury
  • Discrimination Is Breaking People's Hearts

  • Mammals' Perseverance, Fast Radio Bursts and Health Justice

  • Redwoods Grow Weird Leaves to Suck Water from Air

  • New 'Ionogels' Are Tough, Stretchable and Easy to Make

  • Your Body May Be Able to Repair Its Arthritic Joints with Help from Drugs or Surgery

  • Poem: 'Vaulted Seeds,' after the Svalbard Global Seed Vault
  • Most of Us Combine Personality Traits from Different Genders
  • Predatory Journals That Publish Shoddy Research Put People's Lives at Risk
Popular Science. Monthly magazine. Should be downloaded around the middle of each month.

Literary review publishing essay-length book reviews and topical articles on politics, literature, history, philosophy, science and the arts by leading writers and thinkers

entry.title: Asian Review of Books: 27 May, 2022
entry.summary: Asian Review of Books: 27 May, 2022 published at 2022-05-27 00:36AM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • “Solo Dance” by Li Kotomi
  • Podcast with Kerry Brown, co-author of “China Through European Eyes: 800 Years of Cultural and Intellectual Encounter”
  • New Book Announcement: “Into the Desert” by Xuemo
  • Here be Dragons: Asian myths and fables inspiring recent Young Adult Literature
  • “Ogata Kōrin: Art in Early Modern Japan” by Frank Feltens
  • New Book Announcement: “Beethoven in Beijing: Stories from the Philadelphia Orchestra’s Historic Journey to China” by Jennifer Lin
  • “Silent Parade” by Keigo Higashino
  • “Chariot of the Sun: An Informal History of a Siamese Family” by Shane Bunnag
  • “The Bangalore Detectives Club” by Harini Nagendra
  • Podcast with Glynne Walley, translator of “Eight Dogs, or ‘Hakkenden’: Part One—An Ill-Considered Jest” by Kyokutei Bakin
In addition to reviewing books about or of relevance to Asia, the Asian Review of Books also features long-format essays by leading Asian writers and thinkers, to providing an unparalleled forum for discussion of key contemporary issues by Asians for Asia and a vehicle of intellectual depth and breadth where leading thinkers can write on the books, arts and ideas of the day. Widely quoted and referenced, with an archive of more than one thousand book reviews, it is the only web resource dedicated to Asian books. And now, with the addition of the new premium content, the Asian Review of Books, is a must-read publication.
